purpose.statement: This module defines a cross-cutting harm framework for government services, user research, and multidisciplinary delivery work.
It makes harm visible, structured, and reusable across roles, templates, graders, and schemas.

reference_block.name: harm-framework-application
reference_block.title: How to apply this framework
reference_block.statement: Use this framework whenever a request concerns government services, research, policy, delivery, live operations, high-stakes change, or any context where people may be harmed by service design, content, process, tooling, research activity, or organisational behaviour.

checklist.entry: Separate harms to service users, research participants, researchers and team members, operational staff, and indirectly affected third parties where relevant.
Identify plausible harm pathways, not just harm labels.
Record warning signs, current mitigations, residual harms, and escalation triggers.
Do not flatten material human harms into generic project risk language.
Where immediate safety, safeguarding, or serious privacy risk is in scope, escalate to accountable human judgement.

reference_block.name: minimum-response-obligations
reference_block.title: Minimum response obligations
reference_block.statement: When the harm framework is material, the assistant should:

checklist.entry: state affected groups or state that they are unknown
identify relevant harm categories by name or by taxonomy ID
distinguish observed harms from speculative harms
state mitigations already present and those still missing
identify residual harms and escalation needs
avoid implying that harm is fully controlled unless evidence is strong and accountable human review is complete

row.definition: People cannot complete the service or research task correctly, in time, or at all because the journey, content, tooling, or method breaks down.

examples.example: A claimant misses a deadline because instructions are unclear.
A user uploads the wrong evidence because labels are confusing.
A participant cannot join a remote session because the platform does not work with assistive technology.
A person abandons an application because progress is not saved.

warning-signs.sign: High abandonment.
Repeated requests for help on the same step.
Users completing tasks incorrectly.
Participants failing onboarding or setup.

mitigations.mitigation: Use plain language and explicit step-by-step instructions.
Reduce unnecessary steps and duplicate evidence requests.
Provide save-and-return, alternative channels, and assisted routes.
Test tools and session access in advance.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Users are blocked from legal or essential entitlements.
Accessibility barriers prevent participation or completion.
Repeated failure suggests systemic design error rather than individual difficulty.

row.definition: People lose money, incur avoidable costs, miss entitlements, or enter a worse financial position because of the service or research setup.

examples.example: A benefit payment is delayed by avoidable evidence loops.
A user pays for printing, scanning, travel, or phone credit just to comply.
A participant loses wages to attend research and the incentive does not cover the loss.
A person is wrongly told they are not eligible and does not claim support.

warning-signs.sign: Users mention debt, missed payments, or emergency borrowing.
Research participation requires time off work or care costs.
High drop-off where tasks carry out-of-pocket cost.

mitigations.mitigation: Minimise compliance costs.
Offer remote or low-cost routes where possible.
Set incentives that reflect time and burden.
Check eligibility and decision content for clarity and error risk.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Likely loss of income, housing stability, or essential support.
Evidence that service design is causing avoidable financial hardship.
Incentive or reimbursement arrangements are materially unfair.

row.definition: People are asked to spend excessive time, attention, effort, or emotional labour to access services or participate in research.

examples.example: A user must repeat the same information across channels.
A participant spends most of the session on consent, setup, and troubleshooting.
A person with cognitive fatigue cannot complete a long form in one sitting.
Researchers manually redact large volumes of sensitive material because the process was poorly designed.

warning-signs.sign: Long call times, repeated handoffs, and high recontact rates.
Participants report exhaustion or frustration before the main task begins.
Teams rely on manual workarounds to make the process function.

mitigations.mitigation: Design for minimum viable burden.
Remove duplicate asks and simplify journeys.
Allow breaks, pause-and-return, and asynchronous options.
Plan research operations properly rather than offloading effort onto staff or participants.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Burden prevents access for vulnerable or time-poor groups.
Research design depends on unreasonable participant or staff effort.
Operational burden creates repeated safeguarding or data-handling failures.

row.definition: People experience distress, fear, shame, panic, hopelessness, retraumatisation, or sustained emotional exhaustion during or after service use or research participation.

examples.example: A decision letter feels accusatory or dehumanising.
A participant is asked to relive abuse, bereavement, detention, or crisis without preparation.
A user fears they will lose support because a message is unclear.
A moderator continues probing after visible distress.

warning-signs.sign: Crying, shutdown, dissociation, agitation, or visible distress.
Participants asking whether they are in trouble.
Users describing shame, panic, or loss of sleep after contact.
Researchers showing signs of emotional overload.

mitigations.mitigation: Use trauma-informed methods and careful content design.
Warn in advance about sensitive topics.
Allow skip, pause, and stop without penalty.
Provide debriefs, signposting, and moderator training.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Active distress during a session or interaction.
Any sign of self-harm, suicidal ideation, abuse, or immediate vulnerability.
Repeated distress patterns linked to the same service or research activity.

row.definition: People feel disbelieved, judged, stereotyped, humiliated, surveilled, or treated as less worthy of respect or agency.

examples.example: Service content assumes fraud or bad faith by default.
A participant is treated as a data source rather than a person.
A disabled user is offered a visibly second-class workaround.
A trans participant is repeatedly misgendered.

warning-signs.sign: People say the service feels hostile, cold, or shaming.
Users apologise excessively for needing help.
Participants withdraw after feeling judged or exposed.

mitigations.mitigation: Use respectful tone and person-centred language.
Remove default suspicion where not required by policy or law.
Train moderators and service staff on respectful handling of identity and lived experience.
Design adjustments that preserve dignity, not just access.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Repeated complaints of shaming, humiliation, bias, or dehumanising treatment.
Material risk of discriminatory treatment.
Evidence that policy or content is systematically eroding trust and dignity.

row.definition: People are prevented from using the service or participating in research, or are disadvantaged within it, because of disability, language, literacy, digital exclusion, cognitive load, or other access barriers.

examples.example: A form does not work with a screen reader.
A service relies on complex English with no interpretation route.
A participant without stable internet is silently excluded from remote research.
A neurodivergent participant is expected to cope with fast, ambiguous questioning without support.

warning-signs.sign: Certain groups fail or drop out at much higher rates.
Repeated requests for alternative formats or adjustments.
Research samples skew toward easier-to-reach groups only.

mitigations.mitigation: Design to WCAG and inclusive research standards.
Offer reasonable adjustments, interpretation, and channel choice.
Recruit beyond digitally confident groups.
Test with people who face real access barriers.

escalation-triggers.trigger: People cannot access statutory or essential services because of design barriers.
Accessibility issues create discriminatory exclusion.
Teams are proceeding without adjustments in a context where they are clearly needed.

row.definition: Personal or sensitive data is over-collected, mishandled, exposed, misunderstood, or used in ways that create fear, stigma, or downstream consequences.

examples.example: A transcript contains identifiable details and is shared too widely.
A service asks for more sensitive information than is necessary.
A participant does not fully understand that a session is being recorded.
A supposedly anonymised quote is still recognisable internally.

warning-signs.sign: Unclear consent for recording or note sharing.
Excessive data fields with weak rationale.
Researchers or teams using informal storage or uncontrolled sharing.
Participants asking who will see their information.

mitigations.mitigation: Collect the minimum necessary data.
Explain recording, storage, access, and retention clearly.
Use secure storage and controlled access.
Redact or aggregate identifiable material before wider circulation.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Possible personal data breach or unsafe disclosure.
Use of sensitive data without a clear lawful and ethical basis.
Participants reasonably fear case linkage, surveillance, or exposure.

row.definition: People are not able to choose freely, understand what they are agreeing to, or exercise meaningful control over participation, disclosure, or withdrawal.

examples.example: A participant thinks research participation may affect their case outcome.
Consent language is technically correct but practically incomprehensible.
A moderator continues after a participant signals discomfort.
A user feels unable to decline because the request comes from a powerful institution.

warning-signs.sign: Participants asking whether they have to take part.
Unclear withdrawal routes.
Consent obtained quickly with limited understanding checks.
High agreement paired with visible uncertainty or fear.

mitigations.mitigation: Use plain-language consent and comprehension checks.
State clearly that refusal or withdrawal carries no service penalty.
Allow skipping questions and ending participation easily.
Avoid recruitment or framing that exploits institutional power.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Any evidence of coerced, pressured, or misunderstood consent.
Participants believing service outcomes depend on participation.
Use of methods that rely on implied or overly weak consent in a high-stakes context.

row.definition: People are exposed to or disclose abuse, neglect, exploitation, coercion, self-harm risk, suicidal ideation, or other immediate vulnerability requiring protective action.

examples.example: A participant discloses domestic abuse and the team has no clear response route.
A session takes place while a controlling partner may be present.
A vulnerable adult or child is involved without adequate protections.
A service communication increases risk because another person monitors access to the account or device.

warning-signs.sign: Fearful or guarded behaviour.
References to being watched, controlled, hurt, or unable to speak freely.
Disclosures of abuse, neglect, self-harm, or suicidal thinking.
Signs that the participant is not safe where they are.

mitigations.mitigation: Have a clear safeguarding protocol before fieldwork begins.
Train staff on immediate response and escalation.
Use safe contact methods and check whether the person can speak freely.
Provide signposting and protective follow-up routes where appropriate.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Any disclosure or credible sign of abuse, neglect, coercion, self-harm, or suicidal ideation.
Any indication that a child or vulnerable adult may be at risk.
Any immediate safety risk to participant, user, researcher, or others.

row.definition: People face bodily risk, unsafe environments, health risk, or preventable exposure to danger during service delivery or research activity.

examples.example: A researcher travels alone to an unfamiliar location with no check-in protocol.
A participant is asked to attend an inaccessible or unsafe venue.
A home visit includes risk from aggression, unsafe buildings, or animals.
A participant with health needs is kept in a long session without breaks.

warning-signs.sign: Lone working without route planning or contact checks.
Unsafe buildings, volatile locations, or hostile in-person contexts.
Participants reporting discomfort, fatigue, or inability to continue safely.

mitigations.mitigation: Use fieldwork risk assessments and lone-working protocols.
Choose safe venues and remote alternatives where appropriate.
Allow breaks and accessibility adjustments.
Stop sessions where physical safety is uncertain.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Any credible threat of violence or unsafe environment.
Research setup exposes staff or participants to unmanaged risk.
Immediate health or safety concern arises during contact.

row.definition: People experience harm to family relationships, household safety, community standing, employment, or social reputation because of service or research contact.

examples.example: A government letter seen by others reveals sensitive circumstances.
A participant discussing immigration status or debt is overheard at home.
A community is framed negatively in published findings.
A process creates avoidable conflict between a person and their carer or family.

warning-signs.sign: Participants ask for discretion about timings, channels, or labels.
Users mention stigma, shame, or fear of others finding out.
Research outputs risk identifying small groups or locations.

mitigations.mitigation: Use discreet communications and channel choices.
Check whether the person can speak privately.
Avoid stigmatising framing in findings and outputs.
Minimise identifying detail for small populations.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Likely exposure creates household, employment, or community risk.
Research or service contact may worsen abuse, coercion, or stigma.
Findings or outputs create material reputational harm to a protected or vulnerable group.

row.definition: Some groups experience higher failure, burden, suspicion, exclusion, or distress because design choices interact unfairly with protected characteristics, poverty, trauma, language, or other vulnerabilities.

examples.example: A process works worse for people with certain accents, names, or communication styles.
Shift workers, carers, or people with unstable housing face much higher burden.
Research samples exclude digitally excluded groups, making the service appear easier than it is.
Minoritised staff face greater hostility during fieldwork.

warning-signs.sign: Disproportionate complaints, failures, or drop-off by group.
Evidence that standard routes assume a narrow norm.
Recruitment and synthesis erase harder-to-reach experiences.

mitigations.mitigation: Run equality and inclusion checks early and repeatedly.
Recruit and test with diverse and affected groups.
Disaggregate data where lawful and appropriate.
Build alternative routes and adjustments into the default design.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Plausible discriminatory effect or disproportionate harm to protected groups.
Evidence that decisions are being made on skewed or exclusionary research.
Material equality issues cannot be mitigated within current design constraints.

row.definition: People’s experiences are flattened, distorted, extracted, or used in ways that misrepresent meaning, erase context, or justify actions they would not recognise.

examples.example: A participant quote is used to support a policy conclusion they would reject.
A complex lived experience is reduced to a simplistic user need.
A community is described mainly in deficit terms.
Distressing stories are used performatively without meaningful response.

warning-signs.sign: Single quotes carry too much argumentative weight.
Findings strip out social, policy, or trauma context.
Stakeholders cherry-pick evidence to confirm pre-made decisions.

mitigations.mitigation: Use careful synthesis with traceability from evidence to finding.
State limits, uncertainty, and context explicitly.
Avoid over-generalising from small or skewed samples.
Frame participants as knowledgeable agents, not deficits.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Findings are being edited or used in a misleading way.
Research outputs create clear reputational, policy, or equality harm through distortion.
Teams are using evidence extraction to legitimise an unsafe direction.

row.definition: People are led to expect personal benefit, case resolution, rapid service change, or meaningful influence that the service or research cannot realistically provide.

examples.example: A participant thinks sharing their story may improve their case outcome.
A pilot is described in a way that implies support exists when it does not.
Researchers overstate how much impact the session will have.
Teams invite contributions after key decisions have already been made.

warning-signs.sign: Participants ask whether this will change their case.
Users believe they are entering a support route when they are not.
Recruitment or service content uses aspirational language without clear limits.

mitigations.mitigation: Set accurate expectations from the start.
Explain clearly what will and will not happen as a result of participation.
Avoid manipulative framing to boost recruitment or compliance.
Close the loop honestly where possible.

escalation-triggers.trigger: People are making decisions based on a mistaken belief about service outcomes or research influence.
Recruitment or service messaging is materially misleading.
Trust damage is likely because expectations have been inflated.

row.definition: Harm builds over time through repetition, repeated retelling, sustained burden, or long exposure to distressing or degrading processes.

examples.example: A user must recount traumatic events to multiple teams.
A participant is repeatedly recruited into sensitive studies because they are responsive.
Researchers moderate distressing sessions week after week without decompression.
Staff review large volumes of traumatic material without support.

warning-signs.sign: Users say they are exhausted by the process rather than any one step.
Same people or communities are repeatedly asked to carry research burden.
Teams normalise distress because it is frequent.

mitigations.mitigation: Reduce repeated asks and reuse information appropriately.
Track participant burden over time.
Rotate staff, cap exposure, and schedule decompression.
Review the whole journey, not only single interactions.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Patterns of harm are emerging across repeated contacts.
Teams or participants show signs of sustained overload or secondary trauma.
The organisation is normalising harmful burden as standard practice.

row.definition: Researchers, note-takers, designers, analysts, delivery staff, and other team members are harmed through distressing content, unsafe fieldwork, hostility, burnout, harassment, or moral injury.

examples.example: Vicarious trauma from repeated exposure to traumatic participant accounts.
Hostility, sexual harassment, racism, or intimidation during fieldwork.
Lone-working anxiety or unsafe travel during visits.
Moral injury when staff are pressured to ignore or soften evidence of harm.
Burnout caused by poorly paced, high-intensity research schedules.

warning-signs.sign: Sleep disruption, emotional blunting, hypervigilance, or avoidance.
Staff reporting dread, exhaustion, or fear before sessions.
High sickness, withdrawal, or reluctance to continue fieldwork.
Team conflict where ethical concerns are dismissed as blockage.

mitigations.mitigation: Use researcher safety plans, lone-working protocols, and clear stop rules.
Train teams on hostile situations and distress handling.
Provide supervision, debriefs, workload limits, and support routes.
Make it safe to escalate ethics, safeguarding, and delivery concerns.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Threats, harassment, unsafe field conditions, or credible fear.
Signs of vicarious trauma, burnout, or moral injury affecting staff wellbeing.
Pressure from leadership to proceed despite clear harm concerns.

row.definition: Service users, participants, staff, or communities lose trust in the fairness, safety, or legitimacy of the service or research process, reducing future engagement and increasing downstream harm.

examples.example: Users stop seeking help because the process felt degrading or unsafe.
Participants warn others not to engage with research because it felt extractive.
Communities see the department as collecting stories without change.
Staff become risk-averse and avoid meaningful engagement after poorly handled incidents.

warning-signs.sign: Falling engagement and rising refusal.
Community scepticism about motives, fairness, or follow-through.
Repeated complaints about extractive or performative research.

mitigations.mitigation: Act visibly on evidence where possible.
Explain decisions and limits honestly.
Use transparent, respectful, and proportionate methods.
Close the loop with participants and affected communities.

escalation-triggers.trigger: Trust loss is likely to reduce access to essential services or future participation.
Research or service practice is becoming publicly or internally seen as extractive or unsafe.
Legitimacy concerns materially affect delivery, policy confidence, or community cooperation.
